workMixDesign.sourceDerivedSummary: Entry/junior residential architecture is dominated by digital drawing/model artifacts, cognitive coordination, written documentation, and focused client/team communication. Site and field constraints matter, but this scenario handles them through team verification replies and schematic constraints rather than physical field work.

sourceEvidence: O*NET includes site visits/inspections and construction coordination. [onet]
Junior site visit discussions emphasize observing, comparing to drawings, and escalating. [reddit:1c0xx7n]
physicalProceduralTool.simulatorImplication: Represent site conditions through Riley's verification reply and follow-on schematic constraints, not a physical playground.

sourceEvidence: O*NET lists CAD/BIM, drawings, specifications, email, and documentation tools. [onet]
Architect threads describe daily Revit/CAD production and redline pickup. [reddit:12eo2up][reddit:17zcdma][reddit:14b6qjh]
digitalToolArtifactWork.simulatorImplication: Most active scenes use realistic desk work surfaces.

sourceEvidence: Architects synthesize client needs, code, site, budget, and constructability. [reddit:15cyslv]
Junior mistakes come from editing without understanding downstream impacts. [reddit:12xw9j5]
cognitiveAnalysisDecision.simulatorImplication: Tasks require issue spotting and uncertainty handling.

sourceEvidence: O*NET emphasizes written documents and specifications. [onet]
Junior advice emphasizes organized notes and concise questions. [reddit:159xtnc][reddit:12p6n9p]
writtenDocumentationArtifact.simulatorImplication: Require an A101/A201 redline click board, prep notes, a verify resolution summary, and a Revit-like schematic option study.

sourceEvidence: Residential architects explain changes and tradeoffs to clients. [reddit:13yf1i4][reddit:1alviwh]
Architecture days include email, meetings, redlines, and interruptions. [reddit:1hh974y][reddit:17mwbky]
passiveMonitoringWaitingContextSwitching.simulatorImplication: Use Slack-style coordination replies, client call pressure, and deadline pressure.

workMixDesign.simulatorPlan: Active scenes: Slack MCQ with redirects, A101/A201 redline click board, client prep, client voice meeting, verify reply review, and a Revit-like schematic option study.
workMixDesign.activeSceneMixRationale: Six active scenes require constructed work or action; MCQ is limited and consequence-routed.

intro.title: Welcome to Harbor & Ash Studio
intro.content: You are a junior architectural designer supporting a residential addition permit package.
intro.imageBrief: First-person residential architecture studio desk with laptop dashboard, redlines, trace paper, plans, and material samples.

maple_street_inbox.title: Maple Street Inbox
maple_street_inbox.content: Maya catches you before the morning Maple Street work starts and gives you the short version.
maple_street_inbox.imageBrief: First-person view at a residential architecture studio desk before redline pickup and homeowner call prep tasks. The scene shows trace paper, marked-up plans, a site survey sheet, zoning notes, a laptop with generic unreadable project-message shapes, pencils, and material samples. Keep the mood focused and realistic; do not render readable document text, emails, or answer text.

coworkerRecap.fallback: I do not want to make up extra project facts here. Stick with the handoff I just gave you and keep the open items labeled.

turns.coworkerLine: Quick Maple Street download before Dana's homeowner call. Dana and Luis Moreno are asking whether the rear addition can give them a brighter kitchen, a mudroom, and a second-floor bedroom suite. Dana is calling in at 9:30, so I need a clean first pass before anyone starts treating the model like it already answers everything.
answerFacts: Maple Street is a residential rear-addition project for Dana and Luis Moreno.
The owners want a brighter kitchen, a mudroom, and a second-floor bedroom suite.
Dana has a 9:30 homeowner clarification call.
Maya needs a first pass before the model is treated as settled.

turns.coworkerLine: The important thing is that the set has real open questions, not just cleanup. The rear setback is reading short against the zoning minimum, lot coverage may be over, the west bedroom window needs a privacy call, and there are drawing/field coordination issues around the window tags, the maple tree, and the existing basement window and utility meter.
answerFacts: The rear setback appears short against the zoning minimum.
Lot coverage may be over the zoning maximum.
The west bedroom window needs a privacy decision.
The maple tree, existing basement window, and utility meter are field or site coordination concerns.

turns.coworkerLine: So before Dana calls in, I need a first pass that keeps the messy parts visible. Some items are client preferences, some are drawing coordination, and some may need zoning or field verification. Use the first hour in a way that helps us sort those apart before anyone commits to a direction.
answerFacts: The useful first move is to map client goals against zoning, redlines, drawing gaps, and questions.
The player should not sketch a fresh option first.
The player should not promise Dana that the setback is fine.
The player should not wait for Owen and Riley before writing anything.
Known facts should be separated from items that need verification.

first_move.title: Choose Your First Move
first_move.content:
Maya asks how you will use the first hour before Dana's 9:30 call.

The previous chat with Maya is available in View Briefing.
first_move.prompt: Choose the Slack reply you would send.

slackMessages.content: Dana's 9:30 client call is coming up. What are you doing first? I need judgment, not overconfidence.

options.label: I will sketch a better option first so the call has a fresh design direction.
I will map client goals against zoning, redlines, drawing gaps, and questions for you or the client.
I will tell the client the rear addition should be fine because the setback is basically at the minimum.
I will wait for Owen and Riley to answer everything before I write anything.

continuity: Later scenes reference this work where realistic.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Visible source artifact -> student action -> workplace output -> rubric evidence.

redirect_sketch.title: Consequence: Design Too Soon
redirect_sketch.content: Maya redirects you: sketching before checking setbacks, lot coverage, and redlines creates rework.
redirect_sketch.imageBrief: Slack consequence scene in the studio showing Maya redirecting the junior architect.

redirect_constraints.title: Consequence: Useful First Pass
redirect_constraints.content: Maya agrees with the direction: separate clean drafting fixes from anything that needs verification before the Dana call.
redirect_constraints.imageBrief: Slack consequence scene in the studio showing Maya confirming the junior architect's careful first pass.

redirect_promise.title: Consequence: Overpromising Risk
redirect_promise.content: Maya stops you before the client hears that. The drawings still show setback and lot-coverage uncertainty.
redirect_promise.imageBrief: Slack consequence scene in the studio showing Maya redirecting the junior architect.

redirect_wait.title: Consequence: Waiting Stalls the Team
redirect_wait.content: Maya reminds you that incomplete information is normal. She wants a useful first pass with unknowns labeled.
redirect_wait.imageBrief: Slack consequence scene in the studio showing Maya redirecting the junior architect.

redline_note.title: Pick Up the A101/A201 Redlines
redline_note.content: Owen wants a quick pickup pass before you draft Dana's call questions or open Revit. Use the pickup rule as your filter, then click each A101/A201 callout as either Fix or Verify based on whether the current set proves the edit or the team needs confirmation before touching the drawings.

categories.label: Fix
categories.description: The set already proves the edit, so it can be drafted now.

categories.label: Verify
categories.description: The edit depends on a client choice, team judgment, field fact, or permit/zoning check.

callouts.title: South-left window tag
callouts.text: A101 tags the south-left window as W-4; A201 shows the same south-left opening as W-1. The schedule sheet lists that opening as W-4.
callouts.owenNote: The schedule sheet gives you the controlling tag, so update the inconsistent A201 tag to W-4 instead of treating it as a design question.

callouts.title: Missing W-10 tag
callouts.text: A201 shows the upper-left window, but the W-10 tag is missing. The schedule sheet includes W-10 for that opening.
callouts.owenNote: The window is already shown and the schedule gives the tag, so add the missing W-10 callout without changing the elevation geometry.

callouts.title: Rear setback short
callouts.text: A101 rear yard dimension reads 24 ft 8 in; local zoning requires a 25 ft minimum rear setback.
callouts.owenNote: Do not draft around this as if the setback is fine. Verify the dimension and zoning path before anyone treats the addition footprint as permit-ready.

callouts.title: Lot coverage over max
callouts.text: Permit worksheet shows lot coverage at 46% before deck revision; zoning max is 45%.
callouts.owenNote: The proposed area may need a footprint or deck change, so verify the worksheet before you update the model or say the layout works.

callouts.title: West bedroom privacy
callouts.text: A201 west bedroom window needs a privacy strategy. Possible solutions include a higher sill, privacy glazing, exterior screening, or a smaller window, but Dana has not chosen a direction yet.
callouts.owenNote: Those are possible privacy strategies, not a selected solution. Ask Dana to choose the daylight/privacy tradeoff before updating the elevation.

callouts.title: Proposed addition area near existing utilities
callouts.text: Proposed addition area is close to an existing basement window and utility meter; the folder has a survey note but no field confirmation.
callouts.owenNote: Treat the proposed addition area as the issue, not just a foundation line. Verify the field condition before the addition footprint or detail assumptions move forward.

redline_note.referenceContent:
Classify each redline by what you can prove before editing.

Fix: use this only when the project folder, redline set, or Schedule tab already gives you the correction. These are coordination items: matching a tag to the schedule, adding a missing tag that the schedule already proves, or cleaning up a sheet reference.

Verify: use this when the next edit depends on a client choice, Owen/Maya direction, field condition, existing utility, missing measurement, zoning limit, permit worksheet, or anything that could become a promise to Dana.

If the item could be a fix but still depends on an unsettled fact, mark Verify.
redline_note.reviewerIntro: I looked over your pickup. The main thing is to only draft what the set already proves, and verify anything that could change the permit path or client promise.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Classify each redline as a fix that can be drafted now or a verification item that should not be edited yet.
continuity: Later scenes reference this work where realistic.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Visible source artifact -> student action -> workplace output -> rubric evidence.

client_prep.title: Prepare Dana Client Call Questions
client_prep.content: Use the Verify Redlines tab and Dana Call Notes to draft a short question list for Dana's 9:30 homeowner clarification call.

appTabs.label: Dana Call Notes
appTabs.heading: Maya's pre-call notes
appTabs.content:
- Dana wants the kitchen to feel brighter, but she does not want the addition to grow beyond the current footprint.
- Luis wants the mudroom to handle shoes, coats, and sports gear without crowding the kitchen path.
- Dana is worried about how construction will affect daily routines, especially access to the kitchen during the work.

cards.title: Rear setback short
cards.body: A101 rear yard dimension reads 24 ft 8 in; local zoning requires a 25 ft minimum rear setback.

cards.title: Lot coverage over max
cards.body: Permit worksheet shows lot coverage at 46% before deck revision; zoning max is 45%.

cards.title: West bedroom privacy
cards.body: A201 west bedroom window needs a privacy strategy. Possible solutions include a higher sill, privacy glazing, exterior screening, or a smaller window, but Dana has not chosen a direction yet.

cards.title: Proposed addition area near existing utilities
cards.body: Proposed addition area is close to an existing basement window and utility meter; the folder has a survey note but no field confirmation.

client_prep.prompt: Write 3 questions for Dana: one from the Verify Redlines tab item that needs Dana's choice, and two from Dana Call Notes. Ask about homeowner choices or priorities only; do not turn zoning, field-condition, or drawing-coordination issues into promises.

continuity: Later scenes reference this work where realistic.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Visible source artifact -> student action -> workplace output -> rubric evidence.

client_prep.referenceContent:
Use the Verify Redlines tab and Dana Call Notes as your source.

Client-facing: ask Dana about homeowner choices and priorities only. In the Verify Redlines tab, use the item that depends on Dana's choice as one client question. Dana Call Notes should provide the other questions about kitchen brightness, mudroom storage, or construction disruption.

Team/field-facing: do not ask Dana to solve rear setback, lot coverage, drawing tag coordination, generic foundation detail, or utility/basement-window field conditions. Carry those as Owen/Maya/Riley verification items unless the schedule already proves a simple tag fix.

client_call.title: Client Call With Dana Moreno
client_call.content: Use your prep notes to clarify priorities and explain constraints in plain language.
client_call.playerGoal: In 5 turns, ask Dana three decision-useful homeowner questions and get answers specific enough to guide the design team. Use your prep as a starting point, but a better client-facing question is acceptable. If Dana gives an answer that is too vague to guide a design decision, ask a focused follow-up. Do not promise zoning, lot coverage, cost, or field conditions.
client_call.endpoint: End after clarifying priorities and next steps without promising approval or exact cost.
client_call.successCriteria: Ask three decision-useful questions, follow up when Dana's answer is too vague, use plain language, avoid jargon and overpromising, and summarize next steps.
client_call.prepReferenceContent: Dana wants a brighter kitchen without expanding beyond the current footprint. Luis wants mudroom storage that does not crowd the kitchen path. Dana is worried about kitchen access during construction. Rear setback, lot coverage, and field conditions still need team verification.
client_call.goalPrompt: You are Dana Moreno, the homeowner client. The student should ask three decision-useful homeowner questions; use their prep note only as context, and accept a better client-facing question if it helps the design team make a decision. Answer the student's actual questions instead of forcing a fixed topic list. Exactly once, when a question can plausibly receive an incomplete homeowner answer, give a vague answer that is not specific enough to guide a design decision. If the student asks a focused follow-up, provide a concrete preference or priority. If the student asks about the west bedroom privacy/daylight tradeoff, Dana prefers translucent or privacy glass because it improves privacy while still letting daylight into the room; say this as a practical homeowner preference, not a technical glazing specification. If the student overpromises zoning approval, lot coverage, exact cost, or field conditions, ask what is actually known or verified. After the student has asked three useful questions and handled any needed follow-up, conclude with: "Thanks, that gives me a clearer picture. Please send Maya the summary and next steps." Do not open new topics after that.

initialMessages.content: Thanks for talking this through with me. I really want the kitchen to feel brighter, but I don't want this to turn into a much bigger project than we expected.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Conduct a spoken homeowner clarification call.
continuity: Later scenes reference this work where realistic.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Visible source artifact -> student action -> workplace output -> rubric evidence.

client_call.imageBrief: Remote client call with Dana Moreno in a home kitchen under renovation and student prep notes visible.

review_verify_replies.title: Review Verify Replies
review_verify_replies.content: Owen, Riley, and Maya have replied on the redlines you marked Verify. Read the replies, then write one coordination summary for the schematic option work. Treat these as constraints for today's study, not final permit approval.

appTabs.label: Verify Replies
appTabs.heading: #maple-street-addition
appTabs.content: Use these replies to translate the Verify Redlines into schematic constraints. The items are resolved enough for the next option study, but they are not permit signoff.

cards.title: Rear setback

originalRedline.title: Rear setback short
originalRedline.body: A101 rear yard dimension reads 24 ft 8 in; local zoning requires a 25 ft minimum rear setback.

cards.body: Hold the rear setback at 25 ft minimum in the schematic option. Do not describe the footprint as permit-approved; this is the minimum constraint to test against.

cards.title: Lot coverage

originalRedline.title: Lot coverage over max
originalRedline.body: Permit worksheet shows lot coverage at 46% before deck revision; zoning max is 45%.

cards.body: Keep lot coverage at or below 45%. If the option pushes over that threshold, reduce the footprint before sending the study back.

cards.title: West bedroom privacy

originalRedline.title: West bedroom privacy
originalRedline.body: A201 west bedroom window needs a privacy strategy. Possible solutions include a higher sill, privacy glazing, exterior screening, or a smaller window, but Dana has not chosen a direction yet.

cards.body: Dana wants privacy without losing daylight. Use translucent or privacy glass as the west bedroom direction because it improves privacy while still allowing daylight into the room.

cards.title: Existing utilities and basement window

originalRedline.title: Proposed addition area near existing utilities
originalRedline.body: Proposed addition area is close to an existing basement window and utility meter; the folder has a survey note but no field confirmation.

cards.body: Keep the right-rear utility and basement-window clear zone open. Do not place the addition footprint into that area during the schematic option study.

review_verify_replies.prompt: Write one concise coordination summary for the schematic option. Include the 25 ft rear setback, 45% lot coverage limit, translucent/privacy glass direction, and right-rear utility/basement-window clear zone.

fields.label: Summary for schematic option
fields.placeholder: Create a concise note: capture the constraints to carry into the schematic option, not permit approval.

inputArtifacts.label: Verify replies from Owen, Riley, and Maya

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Read resolved verify replies and write one concise coordination summary for the schematic option.
continuity: The schematic option study should carry forward the resolved setback, lot coverage, privacy, and utility-clear constraints.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Verify replies -> coordination summary -> schematic design constraints/rubric evidence.

schematic_option_study.title: Create the Maple Street Schematic Option
schematic_option_study.content: Maya needs a quick schematic option that translates Dana's priorities into a plan move while respecting the resolved verify replies: rear setback, lot coverage, west-window privacy, and the right-rear utility clear zone.
schematic_option_study.prompt: Use the Revit-like workspace to adjust the rear addition footprint, place Level 1 kitchen/mudroom tags, place the Level 2 bedroom-suite tag, choose Translucent glass in the West Elevation view, review the resolved Verify Replies constraints, keep the right-rear utility clear zone open, watch the live constraint check, and write a short design note for Maya/Owen.
schematic_option_study.notesPlaceholder: Write 3-5 sentences for Maya/Owen: state the footprint move, where kitchen/mudroom/suite go, why Translucent glass fits Dana's privacy/daylight preference, which resolved constraints are clear for this study, and what still needs review before permit drawings.

inputArtifacts.label: Maple Street design constraints
inputArtifacts.whereVisible: Verify Replies/Reply Summary tabs, constraint check, and drawing image labels

inputArtifacts.label: Revit project views
inputArtifacts.whereVisible: Revit tab with Level 1, Level 2, and West Elevation sidebar views

inputArtifacts.label: Resolved verify replies
inputArtifacts.whereVisible: Reply Summary tab and right-rear utility clear zone overlay

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Create a bounded schematic option by adjusting a plan footprint, placing Level 1 and Level 2 room tags, choosing Translucent glass in the West Elevation view, checking constraints, and writing a design note.

outputArtifact.label: Maple Street schematic option study
outputArtifact.format: Revit-like plan state plus design notes

continuity: The final assessment uses this as the concrete design-production artifact for the day.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Visible constraints and plan canvas -> student design moves -> schematic option artifact -> rubric evidence.

assessment_gate.title: Studio Day Complete
assessment_gate.content: The Maple Street workday is complete. Your work is ready for assessment.
assessment_gate.imageBrief: End-of-day architecture studio desk with a marked-up schematic option, redline sheet, permit checklist, and constraint notes.

grading.title: Assessment
grading.content: Your work will be assessed against the junior residential architect rubric.

final_report.title: Final Report
final_report.content: Review your strengths and next steps as a junior residential architect.

maya_chen.name: Maya Chen
maya_chen.persona: Licensed project architect, calm and direct, protective of technical accuracy. She expects focused questions, careful uncertainty handling, and constraint-aware schematic design notes.

owen_reed.name: Owen Reed
owen_reed.persona: Practical Revit and drawing-set lead who appreciates exact sheet, tag, and detail references.

dana_moreno.name: Dana Moreno
dana_moreno.persona: Budget-conscious homeowner who wants daylight, privacy, and clear next steps without jargon.

riley_patel.name: Riley Patel
riley_patel.persona: Concise field lead who wants the design team to clarify drawings before construction proceeds.

intro.welcomeBody: Spend a day as a junior architectural designer supporting a residential addition at a mid-size studio.

steps.label: Read
steps.content: Get Maya's project handoff, then use focused redline and constraint tasks as the day unfolds.

steps.label: Produce
steps.content: Sort redline pickups, draft prep questions, summarize verify replies, and create a schematic option study.

steps.label: Communicate
steps.content: Clarify priorities with a homeowner client and escalate uncertainty professionally.

glossary.redline: A reviewer markup showing drawing edits, questions, or corrections.
glossary.permit set: The drawing/document package submitted for building department review.
glossary.setback: Required minimum distance between a building and a property line.
glossary.lot coverage: The portion of a lot covered by buildings and certain structures, often limited by zoning.
glossary.revit: A BIM application commonly used to model buildings and produce coordinated drawing sheets.
glossary.RFI: Request for Information; a formal question seeking clarification.

criteria.name: 最初の着手判断
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 制約が多い朝の状況で、クライアント連絡、図面影響、現場確認、期限のリスクを見て最初に扱うべき仕事を選べている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 図面修正タスク
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 赤字指摘の意味を読み取り、A101/A201で根拠がある修正と、確認前に図面へ反映してはいけない項目を区別できている。Owenのレビューを受けて、W-4/W-1不一致やA201のW-10タグ抜けのようなスケジュールで根拠がある作図修正と、セットバック、敷地被覆率、プライバシー、既存設備条件のような確認事項を混同していない。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 顧客通話
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 準備メモだけでなく実際の通話内容として、顧客向けで意思決定に使える3つの質問を聞き、Danaの回答が曖昧なときは具体的な優先条件や選好までフォローアップできている。不確実なセットバック、敷地被覆率、コスト、現場条件を約束せず、確認事項として次の行動に接続している。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、質問の具体性、3問の完了、フォローアップ、過度な約束の回避、または次の行動に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、質問が少ない、顧客の回答を深掘りできない、未確認事項を曖昧に扱う、または約束と確認事項を混同している。0-3: 通話の目的を外す、顧客に聞くべき質問をほとんど聞かない、根拠なく許認可・コスト・現場条件を約束する、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 確認返信の整理
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Owen、Riley、Mayaの確認返信を読み取り、25 ft rear setback、45% lot coverage、translucent/privacy glass、right-rear utility/basement-window clear zoneを、次のschematic optionに使う制約として簡潔に整理できている。これらをpermit approvalや最終解決として過度に扱わず、設計検討用の条件として接続している。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、制約の具体性、相手ごとの返信根拠、設計への接続、または過度な断定の回避に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、確認済み条件を複数落とす、返信の意味を誤る、または設計条件ではなく一般メモとして扱っている。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、確認返信を無視する、permit/cost/field conditionを根拠なく確定扱いする、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 案の比較整理
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 複数案の使いやすさ、採光、構造や設備への影響、顧客条件との整合を比較し、推奨と保留事項を分けて示せている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。
